q: What is a techfin and why is their name the reverse of fintech?
lead: A techfin is a technology platform (like Alibaba, Tencent) that offers financial services second, with the name reversing 'fintech' to signal it is a technology company first.
points: Term coined by Alibaba's Jack Ma in 2016 to describe Alipay.
They begin with e-commerce, social media, or gaming, then add finance.
They expand from payments into money market funds, wealth management, and insurance.
Financial services are enablers or loss leaders to attract and retain platform customers.

q: Why did Alibaba create Alipay and how did this business grow over time? How is Alipay related to Ant Financial, later known as Ant Group?
lead: Alibaba created Alipay in 2004 to build trust between buyers and sellers by holding payments in escrow, filling institutional voids in China's underdeveloped payment system.
points: Grew from 100 million users in 2008 to 300 million by March 2010; launched mobile payments in 2009.
Expanded into money market funds, wealth management, lending, credit scoring, and banking.
In 2014 Alibaba spun off financial units into Ant Financial Services ahead of its US IPO.
Ant Financial was later renamed Ant Group, whose 2020 IPO was pulled by China's central bank.

q: How did the instant messaging and gaming company Tencent move into financial services? What was the first financial product that it launched?
lead: Tencent moved into finance by adding a payment feature to its messaging platform, first launching the proprietary payment service TenPay in 2005 in response to Alipay.
points: TenPay's uptake was slow until WeChat Pay went viral via the 2014 Red Envelope campaign.
WeChat Pay reached nearly 40% of third-party mobile payments by year-end 2018.
Diversified into a money market fund (Licaitong), challenger bank (WeBank), and insurtech (WeSure).
By 2022, WeChat and WeChat Pay had 1.3 billion monthly active users.

q: What is a bigtech and how do they compare in size to the incumbent financial institutions such as banks?
lead: Bigtech refers to North American global technology companies (Amazon, Apple, Facebook, Google) whose stock market capitalization is far larger than the world's largest financial institutions.
points: They dwarf incumbents like VISA, JPMorgan, MasterCard, Bank of America, and ICBC.
Their strength is vast multi-sided platforms, billions of customers, and massive datasets.
In 2022's most valuable brands, bigtech held the top five; JPMorgan was first bank at #24.
Apple's brand value reached US$482 billion, versus banks lagging far behind.

q: What has been Amazon's path into financial services? What financial products does it offer and why?
lead: Amazon built a payments foundation starting in 2007 and expanded through trial-and-error into select financial products that support buyers and sellers on its e-commerce platform.
points: Amazon Pay (2007) provides a digital wallet with no interchange fee.
Amazon Lending (2011) offers merchant loans from $1,000 to $750,000 by invitation.
Amazon Cash (2017) lets unbanked customers add cash deposits via CVS and 7-Eleven.
It builds internally to increase marketplace sales, not to become a conventional bank.

q: How has Apple found success in embedding financial services into its iOS ecosystem?
lead: Apple succeeded by partnering with incumbents to embed banking into its ecosystem and protect its smartphone market share, using financial apps as loss leaders rather than profit sources.
points: Apple Pay (2014) uses NFC, tokenization, and biometric security; reached 507 million users by 2020.
Apple Card (2019), issued by MasterCard and backed by Goldman Sachs, has no fees and 2% cashback.
Apple Card Family (2021) shares credit lines with 3% cashback.
A savings account (2023) at 4.15% interest, backed by Goldman Sachs, was added.

q: What was Facebook's Libra project and what happened to it?
lead: Libra, announced June 2019, was a Facebook-operated stablecoin backed by fiat currencies and US Treasuries, designed for low-cost global money transfers over Messenger and WhatsApp.
points: Launched with 28 partners but notably had no bank partner.
Regulators in the US and Europe attacked it and called for hearings; the G7 formed a taskforce.
Partners dropped out starting with PayPal, then MasterCard, VISA, eBay, and Stripe.
Rebranded as Diem in December 2020, it was shut down in January 2022 and its code sold.

q: What was the experience of Google with its payment product? How did it evolve over time?
lead: Google struggled with product-market fit, relaunching its payment product multiple times while losing ground to Apple Pay and Venmo.
points: Started 2011 with Google Wallet, then Android Pay (2015) and Google Tez in India.
In 2018 it merged Wallet and Android Pay into Google Pay.
By late 2020 it had 150 million users versus Apple Pay's 500+ million.
Abandoned Google Plex bank accounts in October 2021, shifting to B2B focus.

q: What are the three competitive strengths of the techfins and bigtech in financial services?
lead: The three competitive strengths are platform ecosystems and network effects, access to customers and brand recognition, and big data insights and customer experience.
points: Platform ecosystems generate same-side and cross-side network effects that make platforms sticky.
Massive loyal user bases and strong brands, while banks' branch networks and brand trust have eroded.
Proprietary data analyzed with AI and machine learning to understand customer needs before they arise.
The advantage is not the data itself but how it is used to understand a customer's needs.

q: How serious is the threat from techfins and bigtech for incumbent financial institutions? Will banks, insurance companies, and asset managers become obsolete?
lead: The answer is no; the entry of techfins and bigtech into financial services has had mixed success and will not make incumbents obsolete.
points: Chinese techfins succeeded by addressing institutional voids but always worked with domestic banks.
North American bigtech built payment footholds but largely failed to move beyond payments.
Competition has forced banks to become more customer-centric and innovative.
The winning combination will be bigtech-bank partnerships that combine their respective strengths.
